Deliverable: Reduce Mean Time to Resolve Findings for Non-compliant AWS Resources by 50%
Key Achievements: · Resolved bugs that are preventing resource remediation events from being sent to the remediation log.  This data is required for calculating TTR.
Plans:
· Merge change that will differentiate non-compliant alerts from those sent after a resource has been remediated.  This will focus the data in the TTR dashboard on the primary metric (resources which users remediate following an alert)
· Evaluate data quality to ensure calculations are accurate and our methodology is sound. Of note, we need to decide if we should set a maximum value for the number of days a resource has spent in a non-compliant state.  Without a max value, resources that have been non-compliant for a long time might have an outsized impact on the calculation.
· Once the baseline has been established review dashboard with leadership to gain feedback on how to best present metrics and what data should be included.

Key Achievements:
· We completed 46 / 160 story points worth of tech debt which puts us slightly below 30% for the quarter.   

Noteworthy Tech Debt that was resolved in Q2:
- Improved tracking of resource deletions.  We implemented new hooks in AWS accounts that allow us to monitor when resources that fail compliance checks are deleted.   This allows us to ensure findings are removed from the Ballast database and mark certain findings as "manually remediated" so they can be included in our time to remediation calculation.
- Deprecated all usage of Datadog within Ballast.  We no longer need this.
- Resolved 2 GIS findings for Ballast related to service account password rotation (Workforce and OpenSearch)
- Reduced unnecessary API calls through code optimizations and bug fixes.
Plans: · Moved remaining tech debt tickets back to our Tech Debt Epic.  Will continue driving down tech debt as part of our routine workflow throughout FY26.

Deliverable: ABC News On Premise Production Cluster Launch

Key Achievements:
The Unified IaC modules have now been published to the Card Catalog system, marking the go-live of this pivotal part of our deployment strategy.
The key principals of this new approach will enable many of our key projects for improving the deployment experience.  Examples being:

- By having naming overrides as standard for deployed resources UIaC is what will make our Zero Downtime onboarding project viable.  This project aims to onboard infrastructure to the UIaC approach without any downtime.  With Zero Downtime Onboarding, we will also be enabling the migration to UIaC from old modules and bundles, which is critical, since we don't want UIaC to be another standard, it needs to be the only standard, so easy to onboard to.

- In turn, having Zero Downtime Onboarding in place will enable us to take a programatic approach to database upgrades, so that we can speed up the upgrade process through automation where we can have configuration drift during the upgrade that is remediated through Zero Downtime onboarding again at the end of the upgrade.  With a massive upgrade looming in the future (MySQL 8.0 deprecation, which may seem far off, but we've been migrating MySQL 5.7 databases for 2 years now, so a few years is not a lot of time in that regard.  Speeding up upgrades and reducing effort is critical)

- UIaC separates requirements from the core database deployment and deploys extras on top of those core resources.  With extras meaning items like monitoring, that the database doesn't depend on.  As well as reducing the blast radius of changes, this separation will also enable us to take a different approach to how we deploy these parts, with requirements and core deployments needing to be strictly versioned, while we could consider setting up our monitoring in extras in a way where we can deploy monitoring changes faster and at a wider scale than the one-off deployments we need to do now, because of the tight coupling of all the resources at present meaning that any deployment has the risk of destroying critical infrastructure if a configuration change leads to a replacement of a cluster or instance.
Plans: Complete deployment of the GitLab mirroring and bundle build pipeline for UIaC deployments to legacy DMED infrastructure.

Key Achievements:
The Persona Drift Monitoring PoC dashboard has been built and populated with real world data to cover all the MySQL and Postgres personas originally in scope:

- Base
- Critical
- Enhanced Criticality
- Sensitive

Databases are automatically categorized into one of these personas by applying rules to map information available in yellowpages to the resource ARN of the clusters we are looking at - Resource metadata is pulled from Scanner V2.  A mapping comparing the current configuration to the expected configuration is then applied, highlighting differences (drift) as well as the severity of the issues.  Each persona has different criteria for how the data stores should be configured, emphasizing, performance, security and observability based on their unique requirements.  

By combining this classification with the resource metadata from Scanner V2, YP data and the rules for identifying drift we can identify configuration issues at scale, prioritise addressing configuration concerns based on the classification of those issues and track improvement of our deployed fleet of data stores over time.  With the implementation being in a shared library, this functionality can be used by applications that need the data in future, making drift visible quickly and alongside other metadata in tools like The Way.

This PoC is already making important configuration issues visible to our team, and as the implementation is improved the aim will be to have fully automated database configuration audits that will save time and effort and make remediation of configuration issues possible at a scale that was not feasible through manual auditing.
Plans:
Document gaps for the complete drift monitoring solution and next actions to address those gaps.

Key Achievements:
Held kick off meeting with BI team to begin work on migrating from DB2 ODS database to AWS Redshift database with materialized views.

Established that BI team need 3 separate connections to the Apollo ODS Redshift database:
1. AWS Glue (from different AWS account/VPC than Apollo Redshift)
2. SSIS from on-prem (outside AWS)
3. Cognos from on-prm (outside AWS)

For the AWS Glue connection the BI team have no expertise in setting this up so I am taking the lead on this.  So far I have worked with the Apollo AWS Infrastructure Engineers to set up VPC peering between the Apollo Redshift VPC and the BI AWS Glue VPC which is in a different AWS account.  I have also defined how to create a Redshift Managed VPC endpoint in the BI AWS Glue VPC but final configuration of this requires input from the BI team and testing.
Plans:
Having worked with the Apollo AWS Infrastructure Engineers to set up VPC peering between the Apollo Redshift VPC and the BI AWS Glue VPC (which is in a different AWS account)  I have also defined how to create a Redshift Managed VPC endpoint in the BI AWS Glue VPC but final configuration of this requires input from the BI team and testing.

Alongside that we also need to configure and test the necessary IAM role(s) and permissions policies in both accounts that will enable the AWS Glue to Apollo Redshift connection to work and be able to transfer data.  I have another sessions set up for this with the Apollo AWS Infrastructure Engineers later today and following that will set up another call with the BI team to talk them through the configuration as well as documenting it in Confluence.

Deliverable: Vertical and Horizontal Scaling of Jarvis Clusters

Key Achievements:
Jarvis 3.0 Platform Upgrade Phase 2 has delivered significant infrastructure and UI improvements, with critical bugs resolved and enhanced system stability. The team has successfully deployed and validated updates across environments.

- BBG-4 UI now features improved pod and node screens, namespace filtering, and accurate platform version display.
- Obsolete configurations and menu items have been removed, streamlining the user experience.
- Backend and UI bugs (e.g., session handling, offset management) have been fixed, resulting in more reliable operations.
Plans:
The team is focused on ongoing refinements to both UI and backend systems, with a disciplined approach to deployment and validation. Continuous monitoring and feedback cycles are guiding future enhancements.

- Further enhancements to topic update processes and error messaging are underway, with initial versions under team review.
- Planned improvements include more robust payload handling for consumer group calculations.
Problems ☕:
- Lag calculation issues and oversized HTTP headers have been resolved with backend changes and improved logging.
- UI confusion over default vs. metadata-driven values led to changes for clearer data presentation.
- Some bugs required careful coordination of API calls and session management to fix, highlighting the need for ongoing vigilance.

Key Achievements:
Significant improvements have been delivered to the BabyGroot platform, focusing on data freshness and operational flexibility.

- Added a manual Refresh button to the Topics UI, enabling users to update cached data instantly.
- Enhanced the topic load process and update page to support additional attributes, improving data accuracy and user experience.
- All major changes were validated in non-production and successfully deployed to production environments.
Plans:
The team is executing a phased rollout strategy and is committed to ongoing enhancements based on feedback and operational needs.

- Next steps include monitoring the new features and gathering user feedback for further improvements.
- Continuous evaluation of automation opportunities and additional enhancements is planned.
Problems ☕:
- Cache staleness was discovered during cutover, leading to outdated data being displayed; this was addressed with the manual refresh feature.
- Deployment coordination between environments is critical to avoid disruptions.
- Continued monitoring is recommended to ensure no new issues arise post-deployment.

Deliverable: Show contact info in Recent Events View
Key Achievements: https://jira.disney.com/browse/EDS-11160
Plans:
1. Service On-Call Contacts Visualization
Users can click on a service name associated with any event in the timeline view to visualize related on-call contact information.
Primary data source: Contacts should be from PagerDuty based on the service.
Fallback behavior:
If no on-call contact data is available in PagerDuty, it should fallback to the Yellow Page directory to retrieve the service owner contact.
The contact visualization should:
Clearly indicate the source of the data (PagerDuty or Yellow Page).
Label contacts as either ""On-Call"" (from PagerDuty) or ""Service Owner"" (from Yellow Page).
Contact data should be fetched and rendered on-demand. Preloading is not required.
2. Service Owner Org Hierarchy Visualization (Optional – Pending Discussion)
Users may be able to view the organizational hierarchy of a service owner by clicking on a service in the timeline view.
This feature should follow a similar pattern to the Accountability Tree used in the Experience Monitors.
Non-Functional Requirements
Don't make the workflow less efficient, this should be an improvement in identifying contact information.

Deliverable: Realtime Timeline View + recents events v3
Key Achievements:
Launched real-time correlation within the OpsDash timeline view, enabling users to visualize VX case counts, Video Playback Failure trends, and network changes together in a single timeline. This enhancement provides a more comprehensive view of correlated events, empowering faster identification of patterns and root causes. The update directly supports efforts to reduce mean time to incident identification (MTTI) through contextual event linkage and real-time analysis.
Wiki

We have introduced an enhanced filter UI component in the Timeline View as part of Recent Events V3. This enhancement enables users to filter events more effectively, offering clearer insights and a more comprehensive view of the event timeline. Allows users to narrow down the source of the events so they can manually filter out the noise, making it faster and easier to identify the source of an incident using the event data, ultimately reducing the Mean Time to Investigate (MTTI).

We’ve completed the initial functionalities of the real-time Timeline View and deployed it to production, allowing us to share a URL with a limited group of stakeholders for access and feedback. This feature displays near real-time recent events aligned with the timeline to surface the most relevant signals, alerts, and changes as they occur. This enhancement enables users to quickly correlate system behavior with incident impact, improving responsiveness and decision-making during active investigations, ultimately reducing the Mean Time to Investigate (MTTI).

We enhanced the Incident Timeline View with advanced filtering capabilities by adding new filter types and search criteria to the existing implementation. The update introduces additional search options—including Anomalies, Alerts, and Network Changes—and allows users to search across all of these categories using the single unified search bar at the top of the dashboard.

We refactored the implementation to reduce the number of network calls made to Datadog when fetching service alerts for the timeline view. This improvement enhances the overall performance of the timeline view and provides a more efficient and streamlined incident investigation experience.
Plans: Display near real-time recent events aligned with the timeline to surface the most relevant signals, alerts, and changes as they occur. This enhancement enables users to quickly correlate system behavior with incident impact, improving responsiveness and decision-making during active investigations.

Key Achievements: The team had an information sharing session this week demonstrating the different tools we use to monitor and performance tune in the environment. The end result was a plan for this Sprint where we will implement improvements/updates that will serve as the basis for our post-incident data capture tooling as well as improve our proactive tuning.
Plans:
The team will move this effort forward this Sprint on a few fronts:

We will leverage the existing Data Dog dashboard that was built for Vote and modify it with new enhancements as suggested by the team.  The dashboard shows custom OS and SQL Server metrics that will allow us to quickly identify performance issues.

We will also add functionality to ingest SQL Server error logs into DataDog.  This is a pilot for us to verify functionality and display the logs in the new dashboard which will further reduce time spent diagnosing issues.

We will also develop a process that automates enabling Extended Events / Tracing.  The goal will be to speed up deploying these diagnostic tools by making it easier to utilize the code we have in git.

The team also leverages a suite of performance tuning stored procedures which give us a detailed view or performance issues, new index recommendations, unused indexes, etc.  This effort will analyze the latest version of these procedures and once they check out, use SMB to deploy to our servers.  This will ensure the right troubleshooting/performance analysis tools exists and are ready to go when needed.

Once these changes are developed, we will focus on documentation and demos to ensure the entire team understands how to utilize these tools as this will be our standard operating procedure going forward.

Key Achievements:
As we continue to explore the potential of AI, it is critical to ensure the information it presents is both actionable and useful. In collaboration with the Insights & AI team, we've created a new section on the incident detail page to surface predictive incident data based on past incident records. This will be gated behind a feature flag and exposed to a small opt-in group of Support Center operators. We've built a "usefulness" rating feature for each piece of data so they can provide us direct feedback on whether the data was accurate or not. We hope that by leveraging past incident data, we can improve mitigation times for new incidents. Over the next few sprints we will evaluate the responses and roll out the most useful aspects to everyone.

Deliverable: CI for Quackerjack Android in TWDC GitHub Actions
Key Achievements:
Overview
The Client App Delivery team has designated TWDC GitHub Actions as the preferred, “paved path” CI/CD solution for all Android applications under our support.
Repository Migration and Platform Benefits
Adopting TWDC GitHub Actions necessitates migrating the code repository to TWDC GitHub. This transition means that the applications will operate on the Developer Tooling team’s platform, which offers significantly higher uptime, improved reliability, and greater cost efficiency.
Milestone Achievement
As a first step, we have established continuous integration (CI) for the “Quackerjack” Android application using our paved path solution. This milestone advances us toward our broader key result of migrating both D+ Apple and Android applications to the paved path by the end of fiscal year 2026.

Deliverable: Decouple DNote/SAP applications from Repertoire catalog deployment pipeline
Key Achievements:
02/18/26
Status: Completed & Live in Production
We have successfully decoupled the MVP application from legacy DNote (bvmggod) and SAP direct database connections. The system now consumes catalog and repertoire data via the MINT event-driven architecture (Finance Events), establishing a Single Source of Truth (SSOT) for the entire finance ecosystem.
Technical & Strategic Impact:
Data Fidelity & Consistency: Eliminated data mismatches and synchronization latency by aligning MVP with the global DMG Finance Events feed in MINT, ensuring high-fidelity sales recognition.
Platform Modernization: Successfully transitioned from a legacy monolithic "DB-pull" model to a high-throughput, event-driven architecture. This was achieved by deploying dedicated Kafka topics, MongoDBclusters, and containerized microservices hosted on AWS EKS.
Operational Excellence & Reliability: The deployment follows a "No Click-Ops" philosophy, utilizing standardized GitLab CI/CD pipelines for immutable deployments. We’ve integrated full-stack Datadog observability (monitoring and alerting), significantly reducing maintenance overhead and mean-time-to-recovery (MTTR).
Business Continuity: This architectural shift removes direct dependencies on legacy infrastructure, allowing the product catalog (Products, Alternate Titles, and Masters) to be rebuilt dynamically without impacting upstream legacy databases.

Key Achievements: Over a decade in the making, ABC News has long planned to migrate their canonical flagship website from "abcnews.go.com" to "abcnews.com" for improved brand recognition and alignment. As of February 10, 2026 the migration has been completed. ProServ supported this effort sporadically for over 2 years, collaborating with News engineering and the Compliance & Security team on planning, confirmation, and advance setup of CloudFront and WAF configurations. To support the launch, ProServ refactored the CloudFront distribution, deployed redirect rulesets, configured WAF rules, managed DNS changes, and collaborated on operational procedures to safely switch domain names. Due to the long-time usage of "abcnews.go.com" there was significant risk of dependency breakage, including thousands of ABC News articles and content links. Regardless, the launch was smooth and without incident, demonstrating a high level of operational excellence from all parties involved.

Deliverable: Migrate Sports NewRelic alerts from Slack to Teams

Key Achievements:
On December 1, 2025, ESPN's Sportsbot successfully launched to the public, made available to a subset of espn.com users. This service uses OpenAI to enhance the default search capabilities within espn.com (if you enter a question such as "Who won the 1990 World Series" into ESPN's search, instead of performing a literal search and getting articles to parse, you instead get a conversational answer built into the web experience). The ProServe Sports squad worked closely with the application team to architect the cloud resources for this service across both AWS and Azure, and performed virtually all infrastructure work (CI/CD, metrics and monitoring, load testing, regional failover testing, etc) associated with this service to ensure a successful launch.
Next steps are to iterate on the service as new GenAI offerings become available, and expand the current userbase to eventually be 100% of all espn.com users.

Deliverable: Monitoring stack improvements for 12 Disney+, Hulu, ESPN Playback services
Key Achievements:
Feb 5th - Completed

Summary:
Improved the reliability of monitoring across 13 services by moving critical ALB alarms to CloudWatch, so we’re no longer dependent on Datadog being healthy for incident detection. This removes a key single point of failure and avoids periods of alert blindness when Datadog is unstable.

We also brought canary environments up to the same alarm standard as production in both CloudWatch and Datadog, so progressive deployments are now monitored with the same level of safety and visibility as live traffic.

Overall, this makes our incident detection more dependable and our deployments safer across the PlayIO estate.

Key improvements delivered:
- Removed reliance on a single monitoring platform for critical alerts
- Prevented alert gaps when Datadog is slow, degraded, or unavailable
- Made CloudWatch a primary signal for ALB health
- Brought prod-canary alarm coverage in line with production
- Improved visibility and safety during progressive rollouts
- Standardised monitoring improvements across 13 services
- Reduced operational risk during both incidents and deployments
- Increased confidence that we will be alerted when things actually go wrong
Plans: 2 more services to be worked on. This will picked up in the upcoming days.
